author: @kaidwyer
text:
looking at those shafts makes me think of piston engines. Imagine an internal combustion engine with zero articulating bearing surfaces! You could integrate a points ignition system and cam profiles into the rotating cylinder assembly, no chain or belt, and you wouldn’t need a flywheel due to all the rotating mass.

I suppose a real bear of an issue could present itself when trying to seal oil into the mechanism while it is rotating.

author: @greggv8
text: One problem with the hinged rods is that at zero angle the rods could rotate to put the hinges out of line with each other then it would bind. Such a mechanism would either have to be constrained from straightening fully or movement through zero degrees would need to be quite quick and it could never be allowed to hold at zero degrees, at least not while spinning.

author: @MichaelSkinner-e9j
text:
I was thinking for the O Neil Cylinder,  Besides using 5 Foot Diameter Rope/Cable as the thread in a carbon fiber sheet (just scaled up) with mesh and graphene resin as the filler, Pylons obviously need to be interspersed with a network strung all around it.

Pylons need to be interspersed throughout the basket, so that buildings and the like can be plugged in (the Entire Cylinder needs to act as the foundation)

Could Ariel Eckblaws Disks be designed to act as not Only the scaffolding for the "thread" (5 foot diameter steel or graphene carbon fiber/nanotube cables) but also for inserting the Pylons for the buildings?  The "Thread" could be strung throughout the system, with graphene carbon fiber mesh and I was thinking either Concrete or a graphene resin could fill in the spaces, that way the Cables/industrial ropes bear the brunt of the shearing/lateral forces.

Also, Do you think it's possible to design a graphene carbon fiber "hitchhiker?"  Just like the burs in your socks after you walk through a grassy field, could the spaces be filled with graphene carbon fiber or even nanotube "burs" so that you could fill in the spaces with That, and just pour in graphene resin?  That way the "bur concrete" could also flex laterally with everything else and not just become rubble?  It would still stick together with resin, and any resin that itself cracks, you could take out, fill in with more "bur concrete" and it could flex side to side, even IF the Resin or concrete cracked it would still work if it failed.

To test it out, all I need is one section of the shell, essentially a 15 foot or 20 foot section of 5 foot industrial rope (plastic string from recycled bottles and bags are just fine) with the scaffolding, pylons, and support structure, along with the matrix of mesh and concrete or "graphene carbon fiber burs" with graphene resin filling in the spaces of the section of Cylinder.  Then the tolerances can be measured and spun around.

author: @RobertA-hq3vz
text: Its noisy and noise means energy loss, and that energy loss is more than likely causing microfractures to appear in the 3D printed parts. It's hard to imagine those tiny 3D printed parts lasting more than a few days with continuous use. Even with metal parts there's rods moving in and out of those tubes under tension so there's going to be a lot of wear.  I suspect there's a good reason we don't see this joint being actively used and it's probably not because of fashion.

replies.author: @aaronvienot
replies.text: That is precisely the issue. The device is complex and requires multiple moving parts which must be strong enough to take linear and axial loads -- which adds weight, which forces tradeoffs between torque, speed, and efficiency. For most applications there are better ways of transferring power. The main advantage is the use of simple parts, even though there are a lot of them, so it can be built and maintained without complicated machining.

replies.author: @Cooliemasteroz
replies.text: @JehuMcSpooran yes the simple ones are usually the best. A few years back I thought of a new application for an old invention from the original series back in the seventies. Back then the inventor wanted a small car to drive to work in but needed a bigger car for holidays and he didn’t like the awkwardness of a trailer so he came up with a small trailer with one wheel that could swivel like the front wheel of an aircraft which allowed for a connection to the vehicle that  articulated vertically only which meant that the trailer would always keep in line with the car so it was easy to reverse and it was just like having a longer vehicle. A few years back I thought that it would be good to have one to carry a generator to convert a dedicated electric car into a hybrid for longer trips. I reckon it would be good for electric vans that are already short like the VW Caddy and Renault Kangoo.

replies.author: @JehuMcSpooran
replies.text: @Cooliemasteroz That does sound like a good application for that trailer. Do you remember a few years ago when the mouse plague was on, everyone was getting creative with how to mass exterminate them? Well, I suddenly remembered a guy on the show had put a massive fan on the front of his ute when that locust plague happened about 10 years prior. That would have been awesome to deal with the mice. author: @Xero1of1
text:
I feel like this can be combined with a stirling motor somehow. You'd have to find a way to integrate the circular housing into cooling/heating channels with some valves in place to direct the expanded air into the piston that needs to be pushed out to turn the circular housing. Okay, let me think. 

Divide the circular housing in two  along the circumference. Replace the rods with tubes, capped on one end. 

Drill a hole into the side of the tube sort of like a wind instrument. Just one hole though.

 The hot side would be the back half of the circular housing. Block off 2/3 of the back section from the heat so it can only go into one tube at a time. 

Heated gas on back side of circular housing goes into the only exposed tube end, forcing it to extend, this will also cause the piston/tube to turn the housing as it moves. 

Once it clears the heated area, the circular motion pushes the piston/tube out the rest of the way, which is where hole is drilled into the circular housing for the heated gas to escape into the cold side. When the piston/tube is fully extended, the hole drilled in the tube lines up with the hole drilled into the circular housing, allowing the gas to exit. 

The cold side will need to be of higher volume to allow the gas to cool and should also have some sort of heat absorber/exchange with the outside air. As the gas is exiting this tube, the tube after it is getting filled, causing the housing to continue to spin. 

The first piston/tube has now evacuated its gas, and as the circular housing continues to spin, the piston/tube is retracted as it approaches the hot side again.

And then you'd just need a capillary tube to allow the gas to go from the cold side back into the hot side. This tube should be at the bottom of the device.

I think it would work... :D

author: @thephrase22
text: At this time, there is nothing to keep the rods from turning in their slots when there is no bend (straight line, 180 degrees). So when the bend is straight, the joints can turn (or roll). Turned joints cannot be bent anymore. So when going from bent to straight to bent, be sure to not leave it straight very long, otherwise it can’t be bent any further.

author: @jackiemitchell4418
text: At the very moment that 1 of the 3 shafts is reversing direction, it's being powered by the pull and push of the other two shafts equally. It would seem to me that 5 shafts could work drastically better (of course more than 5 would be even better but we need to be able to manufacture simply and inexpensively). These 5 could be easily done within the same area using smaller diameter shafts. That way, at the moment that one shaft is reversing direction, the other 4 shafts would be powering it to a much smoother degree while have less sidewall drag on the shafts (frictional resistance).  Less friction, less wear and tear, and easier manufacturing tolerances.

author: @TimeSurfer206
text:
Ever since the first video on this subject, that eternity since yesterday, I have been pondering this device.
And it struck me, just how easy one could make a supersized one of the Original, non-flexible ones, with pipe and elbows.
We'd definitely want to machine the pipes appropriately for the sleeves. And I am picturing brass sleeves, with teflon coated pipes. In 3/4 Inch (19mm) or equivalent. I wonder what it's load capacity would be.
And, silly question time, which I'm about to Google: Would more shafts allow a smoother transfer?
A: Apparently, the number of shafts is variable.
Oh, another observation: As I was watching the video yesterday, I was looking at the ends of the shaft reciprocating, and pondering ways to harness that.

replies.author: @jondoe598
replies.text: 🤔🤔🤔 yes good idea 😊

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: Good idea, though you might have better luck using fine graphite or graphene on the pipes rather than the teflon.  It would be a great experiment to test them to see which would do a better job of reducing the friction.

replies.author: @smaqdaddy
replies.text: You could have the ends of the rods affect valves that let the gas in for the case of steam or compressed air, or you could have them uncover ports for internal combustion.
